title: ASIC says fund loan valuations lagging 'reality'
full_text:
The corporate regulator says it is concerned that some private credit funds are not properly valuing their loans after an eight-week review revealed the fast-growing sector was entering its first period of significant stress.
The Australian Securities and Investment Commission said the industry was on notice to ensure valuations set at the end of the financial year "were current, accurate and grounded in realistic assumptions", particularly as more difficult economic conditions lead to higher defaults and arrears.
"We are concerned that there are pockets where valuations are lagging that economic reality," ASIC commissioner Simone Constant said in an interview after revealing high-level findings of a snap probe into the sector.
The Australian private credit sector has grown to an estimated $224 billion as institutional and individual investors have chased high yields and stable asset values by investing in funds that loan money to businesses.
But private credit has come under intense regulatory scrutiny after ASIC embarked on a detailed months-long probe, while global worries about loan quality and valuations - particularly of software companies exposed to artificial intelligence - have led individual investors to pull billions of dollars out of funds managed by Blue Owl Capital, Blackstone and Blackrock.
The corporate regulator's latest probe reviewed 52 local private credit funds overseeing $76 billion over an eight-week period to May 14, assessing how those firms were dealing with the weakening economy and withdrawal pressures experienced by global operators.
"There is a slow but certain creep when it comes to credit deterioration," Constant said, adding that there were higher default rates in parts of the market but also changes to terms, which made it difficult to compare.
"Macroeconomic pressures, including inflation, rising costs and supply disruptions are affecting borrower performance," the report said.
ASIC described the end-of-year valuations as "an immediate point of action in private credit and across private markets investments generally".
"If valuations do not reflect current conditions and incorporate verified accurate information, there is a higher risk of misinformation and poor investor outcomes," the regulator said in a statement.
While local credit funds did not face the acute redemption pressures experienced by global players, there had been a slowing in fundraising.
The ASIC review followed a wave of global headlines about elevated redemptions that shook confidence in the sector, as investors fretted over loan valuations and a high exposure to the debt of software companies that were potentially vulnerable to disruption from artificial intelligence.
Some of the country's largest private credit managers, MA Financial and Metrics, sought to assure investors that local funds were not experiencing the same strains as their global peers, which were heavily exposed to software stocks via so-called semi-liquid or evergreen funds.
"Difference is not a defence," Constant said. "Difference in terms of being more focused or concentrated in property is simply difference. It's not a defence against stresses, problems or consumer and investor harm."
While international funds tend to have higher exposure to private companies, local activity is heavily skewed towards real estate development. ASIC said weaker borrowing conditions were increasing the risk that valuations were not being appropriately adjusted.
"In sectors such as property development, current conditions may expose pressure through cost escalation, project delays, soft presales, unsold stock and weaker refinancing conditions," the report said.
Constant said there was "absolutely a risk" that investors in private credit funds could lose money, but the regulator was "not trying to stoke alarm".
"We want to make sure that the risks of private credit are transparent at a system level and at the individual investment level, and that the valuations are credible and robust," she said, adding that the regulator would be increasing its surveillance of boards.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Jonathan Shapiro
heading: Regulation
